Core Explanation: Restaurant POS systems provide strong historical reporting, but limited predictive support for short-term operational planning.
Project Implementation: The project focuses on short-term demand forecasting using real POS data to support staffing, menu preparation, promotional timing, and operational readiness.
Why It Matters: This defines the transition from descriptive reporting to predictive and prescriptive decision support.

Core Explanation: The project evaluates whether temporal, seasonal, weather-related, and operational factors improve short-term restaurant demand forecasting.
Project Implementation: The central hypothesis is that SARIMAX and hybrid forecasting approaches can provide more decision-relevant demand estimates than simple baseline methods.
Why It Matters: This guides the comparison between baseline, SARIMAX, Random Forest, Hybrid SARIMAX + RF, and future conformal prediction.

Core Explanation: The project uses real Twisted Bar POS data, including sales, transactions, orders, items, discounts, refunds, tips, calendar variables, weather indicators, and event features.
Project Implementation: The data was consolidated, cleaned, standardized, aggregated at daily level, aligned with external weather data, and validated for modeling.
Why It Matters: This ensures that the models are built on structured, reliable, and operationally meaningful data.

Core Explanation: The analytical workflow includes exploratory data analysis, feature engineering, baseline forecasting, SARIMAX, Random Forest, Hybrid SARIMAX + RF, KPI forecasting, rolling backtesting, and model comparison.
Project Implementation: Model performance is evaluated using MAE, RMSE, MAPE, Approximate Accuracy, and R².
Why It Matters: This demonstrates a complete applied analytics process from raw data to validated forecasting outputs.

Core Explanation: The Hybrid SARIMAX + Random Forest model achieved the strongest overall forecasting performance, reducing MAPE from 22.48% in the baseline to 16.20%.
Project Implementation: January 2026 showed higher instability, supporting the need for weather-aware and uncertainty-aware forecasting improvements.
Why It Matters: This shows that improvement came from combining time-series structure, feature engineering, and nonlinear residual correction.

Core Explanation: The portal includes model comparison, annual rolling backtesting, final holdout forecast, Random Forest sensitivity analysis, feature engineering summaries, and operational value interpretation.
Project Implementation: The Manager Dashboard translates the analytical results into visual KPIs, trends, forecast charts, and planning insights.
Why It Matters: This makes the project understandable for both technical evaluators and restaurant stakeholders.

Core Explanation: Forecasting can support proactive staffing, menu preparation, inventory readiness, promotion planning, seasonal strategy, and risk-aware decision-making.
Project Implementation: The dashboard helps restaurant managers move from reactive historical reporting to proactive operational planning.
Why It Matters: This is the main practical value added by the project.

Core Explanation: The current MVP focuses on Twisted Bar because it has the most complete dataset. Chile & Agave remains a future extension due to partial data availability.
Project Implementation: Future improvements include conformal prediction intervals, neural network exploration, labor cost KPIs, payroll integration, stronger external variables, and multi-location forecasting.
Why It Matters: This shows methodological transparency and a realistic roadmap for extending the project beyond the current Capstone stage.
